html:
Mynheer Peeperkorn

Mynheer Peeperkorn, an elderly Dutchman, spent some time at House Berghof, that establishment which, in its prospectus, so correctly described itself as ‚Äúinternational.‚Äù Pieter Peeperkorn‚Å†‚Äîsuch was his name, so he called himself, as for instance, ‚ÄúPieter Peeperkorn will now take unto himself a Hollands gin‚Äù‚Å†‚Äîwas a colonial Dutchman, a man from Java, a coffee-planter. His slightly faded nationality is scarcely sufficient ground for introducing him at this late day into our story. God knows we have had racial mixtures aplenty in the famous cure conducted with such many-tongued efficiency by Herr Hofrat Behrens! There was the Egyptian princess who had given the Hofrat the extraordinary coffee-machine and sphinx cigarettes, a sensational person with cropped hair and beringed fingers yellow with nicotine, who went about‚Å†‚Äîexcept at the main meal of the day, for which she made full Parisian toilet‚Å†‚Äîin a sack coat and well-pressed trousers; and who scorned the world of men, to lay hot and heavy, though fitful siege to an insignificant little Romanian Jewess called plain Frau Landauer, while Lawyer Paravant for her royal highness‚Äôs beaux yeux neglected his mathematics and altogether played the fool for love. This princess, in addition to her own colourful personality, had among her little suite a Moorish eunuch, a weak and sickly man, who yet, despite his basic and constitutional lack‚Å†‚Äîupon which Caroline St√∂nr loved to dwell‚Å†‚Äîclung to life more desperately than most, and was quite inconsolable over the conclusions Hofrat Behrens drew from the transparency they made of his dusky inside.

Mynheer Peeperkorn, then, compared with such phenomena, might seem well-nigh colourless. And it is true that this part of our story might, like an earlier chapter, bear the caption ‚ÄúA Newcomer.‚Äù But the reader need not fear that in him another occasion for pedagogic strife has arrived upon the scene. No, Mynheer Peeperkorn was not the man to be the bearer of logical confusion. He was quite a different man, as we shall see. Yet he brought sore dismay and perplexity upon the hero of our tale, as will shortly be very evident.

Mynheer Peeperkorn arrived at the Dorf station by the same evening train as Frau Chauchat. They drove up in the same sleigh to House Berghof, and supped together in the restaurant. The arrival, in short, was not only coincident but concurrent, and continued in that sense, Mynheer taking his place beside the returned wanderer at the ‚Äúgood‚Äù Russian table, opposite the doctor‚Äôs seat‚Å†‚Äîthe place Popoff had occupied, what time he performed his wild and equivocal antics. The companionship troubled our good Hans Castorp‚Å†‚Äîthat it should turn out like this had never entered his mind. The Hofrat, after his own fashion, had announced the day and hour of Clavdia‚Äôs return. ‚ÄúWell, Castorp, old top,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúthere‚Äôs always a reward for faithful waiting. Tomorrow the little puss will be slinking back‚Å†‚ÄîI‚Äôve had a dispatch.‚Äù But not a word that she might not come alone. Perhaps he did not know that she and Peeperkorn were travelling together; at least, he showed surprise when Hans Castorp, the day after, as much as took him to task.

‚ÄúDon‚Äôt know myself where she picked him up,‚Äù he declared. ‚ÄúI take it they met on the return from the Pyrenees. Alas, poor Strephon! Tut, my lad, you‚Äôll have to put up with it, no use pulling a long face. They‚Äôre thick as thieves, it seems, have even their luggage in common. The man‚Äôs larded with money, from what I hear. Retired coffeeking, Malayan valet, plutocratic is no word for it. But he hasn‚Äôt come up here for fun. A catarrhal condition due to alcoholism‚Å†‚Äîand from what I can see he is threatened with tropical fever, malignant, intermittent, you know; protracted, obstinate. You‚Äôll have to be patient with him.‚Äù

‚ÄúDon‚Äôt mention it,‚Äù replied Hans Castorp, loftily. ‚ÄúAnd what about you?‚Äù he said to himself. ‚ÄúI wonder what your feelings are; you didn‚Äôt come off scot-free either, or I miss my guess, you blue-in-the-face widower, with your oil-painting technique. Old dog in the manger! You needn‚Äôt tell me: so far as Peeperkorn is concerned, I‚Äôm certain we‚Äôre companions in misery.‚Äù‚Å†‚Äî‚ÄúQuaint creature,‚Äù he continued aloud, and shrugged. ‚ÄúAn original, certainly. He‚Äôs so lean‚Å†‚Äîyet he‚Äôs robust; that is the impression he makes, at least that‚Äôs the impression I got at breakfast. Lean, and robust, those are the adjectives, I think, though they aren‚Äôt commonly used together. He is certainly tall and broad, and likes to stand with his legs apart and his hands in his trouser pockets‚Å†‚Äîwhich, I observe, are put in running up and down, not like yours and mine and most people‚Äôs of our class. And when he stands there and talks, in his guttural Dutch voice, there‚Äôs something unmistakably robust about him. But he has a sparse whisker, you could almost count the hairs; and his eyes are very small and pale, hardly any colour to them at all. He keeps trying to open them wide, and has made a lot of wrinkles, regular corrugations, that turn up on the temples and run straight across his forehead, and his forehead is high and red, with long wisps of white hair. He wears a clerical waistcoat, but his tailcoat is check. These are the impressions I got this morning.‚Äù

Behrens answered: ‚ÄúI see you‚Äôve taken his number‚Å†‚Äîyou‚Äôre right, too, for you will have to come to terms with his being here.‚Äù

‚ÄúYes, I expect we shall,‚Äù said Hans Castorp. We have left it to him to describe the unlooked-for guest, and he has not come badly off‚Å†‚Äîwe could scarcely add anything essential to the picture. He had a good view; as we know, he had in Clavdia‚Äôs absence moved closer to the ‚Äúgood‚Äù Russian table; the one where he now sat stood parallel with hers, only rather farther away from the verandah door. Both he and Peeperkorn were on the inner and narrow side of their respective tables, and thus, in a way, neighbours, Hans Castorp being slightly in the Dutchman‚Äôs rear, very advantageously placed to observe him, as also to look at the three-quarter view which Frau Chauchat‚Äôs profile presented. We might round out Hans Castorp‚Äôs description by a few notes: as, that the Dutchman‚Äôs nose was large and fleshy, his mouth large too, and bare of moustaches, the lips of irregular shape, as though chapped. His hands were fairly broad, with long, pointed nails; he used them freely as he talked, and he talked almost continuously, though Hans Castorp failed to get his drift. Those adequate, compelling, cleanly attitudes of the hands‚Å†‚Äîso varied, so full of subtle nuances‚Å†‚Äîpossessed a technique like that of an orchestral conductor. He would curve forefinger and thumb to a circle; extend the palm, that was so broad, with nails so pointed, to hush, to caution, to enjoin attention‚Å†‚Äîand then, having by such means led up to some stupendous utterance, produce an anticlimax by saying something his audience could not quite grasp. Yet this, perhaps, was less a disappointment than it was a conversion of expectancy into ecstatic amaze; for the speaking gesture made good what he did not say, and was of itself alone vastly satisfying and diverting. Sometimes, indeed, after leading up to his climax, he left it out altogether. He would lay his hand tenderly on the arm of the young Bulgarian scholar next him, or on Frau Chauchat‚Äôs on the other side; then lift it obliquely for silence, create suspense for what he was about to say, wrinkling high his brows, so that the lines running upwards from the outer corners of his eyes were deepened like those on a mask; he would look down on the cloth before his neighbour‚Äôs place, and from his thick, distorted lips words of the highest import seemed about to issue then, after more pause, he would breathe an outward breath, give up the struggle, nod, as though to say ‚ÄúAs you were,‚Äù and return undelivered to his coffee, which was served to him of extra strength, in his own machine.

After the draught he would proceed thus, choking off with one hand the conversation, making a silence round him, as a conductor hushes the confused sounds of tuning instruments and collects his orchestra to begin a number; mastering at will any situation, for could anything resist that regal head, with its aureole of white hair and its pallid eyes, the great folds of the brows, the long whisker and shaven raw upper lip? They were silent, they looked at him and smiled, they waited, anticipatorily nodding. He spoke.

In rather a low voice he said: ‚ÄúLadies and gentlemen. Very well. Very well indeed. Very. Settled. But will you keep in mind, and‚Å†‚Äînot for one moment‚Å†‚Äînot one moment‚Å†‚Äîlose sight of the fact‚Å†‚Äîbut no more. On this point not another word. What is incumbent upon me to say is not so much‚Å†‚Äîit is in the first place simply this: it is our duty‚Å†‚Äîwe lie under a solemn‚Å†‚Äîan inviolable‚Å†‚ÄîNo! No, ladies and gentlemen! It was not thus‚Å†‚Äîit was not thus that I‚Å†‚Äîhow mistaken to imagine that I‚Å†‚Äîquite right, ladies and gentlemen! Set‚Å†‚Äîtled. Let us drop the subject. I feel we understand each other, and now‚Å†‚Äîto the point!‚Äù

He had said absolutely nothing. But look, manner, and gestures were so peremptory, perfervid, pregnant, that all, even Hans Castorp, were convinced they had heard something of high moment; or, if aware of the total lack of matter and sequence in the speech, certainly never missed it. We wonder how it might appear to a deaf person. Perhaps the impressiveness of what he saw would make him draw an altogether wrong conclusion as to what he might have heard but for his infirmity‚Å†‚Äîand cause him to suffer accordingly. Such people incline to mistrust and bitterness. On the other hand, a young Chinaman at the other end of the table, who possessed too little of the language to understand what had been said, but had yet assiduously listened and looked, clapped his hands and called out: ‚ÄúTr√®s bien, tr√®s bien.‚Äù

And Mynheer Peeperkorn came ‚Äúto the point.‚Äù He drew himself up, swelled his broad chest, buttoned the check frock-coat over the clerical waistcoat; the pose of his white head was regal. He beckoned to a ‚Äúdining-room girl‚Äù‚Å†‚Äîit was the dwarf‚Å†‚Äîand though busily engaged, she at once obeyed his weighty summons, and stood, milk jug and coffeepot in hand, by his chair. She too felt drawn to look at him with an ingratiating smile on her large, old face; she too was rapt by the pallid gaze beneath the deep-wrinkled brow; by the lifted hand, whose thumb and forefinger were joined in an O, while the other three with their lanceolate nails stood stiffly up.

‚ÄúMy child,‚Äù said he, ‚Äúvery well. Very well indeed‚Å†‚Äîvery. You are small‚Å†‚Äîwhat is that to me? On the contrary. I find it a positive good, I thank God, that you are as you are; I thank God you are so small and full of character. What I want of you is also small and full of character. But in the first place, what is your name?‚Äù

She said, smiling and stammering, that her name was Emerentia.

‚ÄúSplendid,‚Äù cried Peeperkorn, throwing himself back in his chair and stretching out his arm toward her. He cried it in the tone of one who would say ‚ÄúWonderful! Is not everything wonderful?‚Äù‚Å†‚Äî‚ÄúMy child,‚Äù he went on, with a perfectly serious face, almost sternly, ‚Äúyou surpass all my expectations. Emerentia! You utter it so modestly‚Å†‚Äîyet, taken with your person, it holds out such boundless possibilities. Beautiful. Worth dwelling upon, communing with in the depths of one‚Äôs‚Å†‚Äîin order to‚Å†‚Äîunderstand me, my child: as a term of endearment‚Å†‚Äîthe pet name. It might be Rentia. Though Emchen would equally warm and fortify the heart‚Å†‚Äîin short, for the moment, I will abide by Emchen. Emchen, then, Emchen my child, attend. A little bread, my love. But hold! Let no misunderstanding come between us‚Å†‚Äîfor in your somewhat over life-size face I seem to read‚Å†‚Äîbread, Renzchen, bread; yet not baker‚Äôs bread, of which in this place we have enough and to spare, in all conceivable forms. Not corn that is baked, my angel, but corn that is burnt‚Å†‚Äîin other words, distilled. Bread of God, bread of sunshine, little pet name; bread for the laving of man‚Äôs weary spirit. But I still have misgivings‚Å†‚Äîwhether the sense of this word I would even consider substituting for it another, the beautiful word ‚Äòcordial‚Äô‚Å†‚Äîif here we did not encounter a new danger, that it might be understood in the ordinary thoughtless sense‚Å†‚ÄîNo more, Rentia. Settled. Set‚Äëtled, and out of the question. Rather would I, in consideration of the debt of honour I acknowledge, right cordially to rejoice your characteristic smallness‚Å†‚Äîa gin, love, and haste thee. A Schiedamer, Emerentia. Bring me one hither.‚Äù

‚ÄúA geneva, sir,‚Äù repeated the dwarf, and spun three times round on herself, seeking a place for her jugs, which she finally deposited on Hans Castorp‚Äôs table, quite near him, obviously not wishing to burden Herr Peeperkorn with the same. She put wings to her feet, and he soon received his desire. The little glass was so full that the ‚Äúbread‚Äù overflowed and bedewed the plate. He took the grain distillation between thumb and middle finger, and held it toward the light. ‚ÄúPieter Peeperkorn,‚Äù he declared, ‚Äúwill now take unto himself a glass of Hollands.‚Äù He appeared to chew the liquid somewhat, then swallowed it down; ‚ÄúAnd now,‚Äù he said, ‚ÄúI look on you all with new eyes.‚Äù He lifted Frau Chauchat‚Äôs hand from the cloth, carried it to his lips and laid it back, letting his own rest for some while upon it.

An odd man, and of great personal weight, though incoherent. The population of the Berghof were enthusiastic over him. It was reported that he had only lately retired from his colonial interests and transferred them to the continent. He was said to have a magnificent house at The Hague, and another at Scheveningen. Frau St√∂hr called him a money magnet (the unhappy woman meant ‚Äúmagnate‚Äù) and indicated the string of pearls Frau Chauchat had worn in the evening since her return to the Berghof. These pearls, Frau St√∂hr considered, were scarcely a token of affection from the trans-Caucasian husband; more likely they came out of the common travelling-trunk. She winked and jerked her head in the direction of Hans Castorp, whose discomfiture she parodied with her mouth drawn down‚Å†‚Äîno, illness and affliction had had no power to refine Caroline St√∂hr; her jeers over the young man‚Äôs disappointment positively went beyond bounds. He preserved his composure, and corrected her blunder, not unadroitly. It was ‚Äúmagnate,‚Äù not ‚Äúmagnet‚Äù she had meant to say, he told her. Money-magnate. But ‚Äúmagnet‚Äù was not so bad after all‚Å†‚Äîcertainly Herr Peeperkorn had a good deal that was attractive about him. The schoolmistress, Fr√§ulein Engelhart, with a wry smile, flushing dully, but not looking at him as she spoke, asked how he liked the new guest. He replied, quite calmly, that he found Mynheer Peeperkorn a ‚Äúblurred personality‚Äù; a personality, that is, undoubtedly, though blurred. The precision of the characterization showed objectivity and poise; it dislodged the schoolmistress from her position. Ferdinand Wehsal, too, made oblique reference to the unexpected circumstances of Frau Chauchat‚Äôs return; and got from Hans Castorp proof that a look may be every whit as telling and unequivocal as the articulate word. ‚ÄúYou paltry wretch,‚Äù said the stare with which Hans Castorp measured the Mannheimer‚Å†‚Äîsaid it without the shadow of a doubt of its meaning. Wehsal understood that look, and pocketed it up; even nodded and showed his bad teeth; but from that time forward he ceased to carry Hans Castorp‚Äôs overcoat, when they went their walks with Naphta, Settembrini, and Ferge.

But dear me, Hans Castorp could carry his own coat, couldn‚Äôt he‚Å†‚Äîand much preferred to; he had only let the poor creature take it now and then out of sheer good feeling. However, there was no doubt everybody in the circle knew that Hans Castorp was hard hit by the wholly unforeseen circumstance, which frustrated all the hopes he had cherished against the return of his carnival partner. It would be putting it even better to say that she had rendered nugatory all his hopes; that, precisely, was the mortifying fact.

His designs had been of the most discreet and delicate, he had meant nothing clumsy or abrupt. He would not even fetch her from the station‚Å†‚Äîwhat a mercy, indeed, he had not thought of doing so! Uncertain whether a woman‚Å†‚Äîupon whom illness had conferred such a degree of freedom‚Å†‚Äîuncertain whether she would even admit the fantastic adventures of a dream dreamed on carnival night, in a foreign tongue to boot! Whether she would even wish in the first instance to be reminded of them. No, there would be no exigence, no clumsy pressing of claims. Admitted that his relations with the slant-eyed sufferer went beyond the limits prescribed by the traditions of the Occident; the uttermost formality of civilization, even for the moment apparent forgetfulness‚Å†‚Äîwas indicated as the suitable procedure. A respectful greeting from table to table‚Å†‚Äîonly that, for the time, no more. A courtly approach as occasion indicated, an easy inquiry after the health of the traveller. The actual meeting would follow in good time, as a reward for his chivalrous reserve.

All this fine feeling, now, had become null and void‚Å†‚ÄîHans Castorp‚Äôs conduct being deprived of choice, and therewith of merit. The presence of Mynheer Peeperkorn effectively disposed of any tactics save utter aloofness. On the evening of the arrival, Hans Castorp had seen from his loge the sleigh come up the winding drive. On the box next the coachman sat the Malayan valet, a yellow little man with a fur collar to his overcoat, and a bowler hat. At the back, his hat over his brows, sat the stranger, beside Clavdia. That night Hans Castorp got little sleep. Next morning he heard for the asking the name of the mysterious new arrival; heard likewise that the two travellers occupied neighbouring suites on the first floor. He was early at breakfast, and sat in his place erect but pale, awaiting the slamming of the glass door. It did not come. Clavdia‚Äôs entrance was noiseless; for Mynheer Peeperkorn closed the door behind her‚Å†‚Äîtall and broad, his white hair flaring above his lofty brow, he followed the familiar gliding tread of his companion, as with head stuck out before her she slipped to her chair. Yes, she was unchanged. Regardless of his programme, Hans Castorp devoured her, with his sleep-weary eyes. There was the red-blond hair, no more elaborately dressed than of yore, wound in the same simple braid about her head; there were the ‚Äúprairie-wolf‚Äôs eyes,‚Äù the rounding neck, the lips that seemed fuller than they actually were, thanks to the prominent cheekbones, which gave the cheeks that exquisite flat or slightly concave look.‚Å†‚ÄîClavdia! he thought, and thrilled. He fixed his eyes on the unexpected guest; not without a toss of the head for the splendid masklike impression the person made; not without summoning a sneer at pretensions which, however justified by present possession, were invalidated by the past‚Å†‚Äîby certain very definite events in the past‚Å†‚Äîfor instance in the field of amateur portraiture. Hans Castorp knew, for had not those events visited himself with justifiable pangs?‚Å†‚ÄîEven her way of turning, before she sat down, to present herself, as it were, to the room, she had as of yore. Mynheer Peeperkorn assisted at the little ceremony, standing behind her while it took place, and then seating himself at Clavdia‚Äôs side.

As for that courtly salute from table to table‚Å†‚Äînothing came of it. Clavdia‚Äôs eyes, when she presented herself, had passed over Hans Castorp‚Äôs person and his whole vicinity, and rested upon the far corner of the room. At the next meal it was the same. And the more meals passed without any response to his gaze than this blank and indifferent passing-over, the more impracticable became the project of the courtly salute. After supper the two travelling-companions sat in the small salon, on the sofa together, surrounded by their tablemates; and Peeperkorn, his magnificent visage flaming against the flashing white of hair and beard, drank out the bottle of red wine he had ordered at table. At each of the main meals he drank one, or two, or two and a half bottles, in addition to the ‚Äúbread‚Äù which he took even at early breakfast. Obviously the system of this kingly man stood in more than common need of moistening. He took in fluid likewise in the form of extra-strong coffee, many times a day, drinking it out of a large cup, even after dinner‚Å†‚Äîor rather, he drank it during dinner, along with the wine. Wine and coffee, Hans Castorp heard him say, were both good for fever‚Å†‚Äîquite aside from their cordial and refreshing properties‚Å†‚Äîvery good against the intermittent tropical fever which had kept him in bed for several hours the second day after he arrived. The Hofrat called it quartan fever: it took the Dutchman about every fourth day, first with a chill, then with a fever, then with a mighty sweat. He was said to have also an inflamed spleen, from the same cause.
